text: Although single-cell and spatial sequencing methods enable simultaneous measurement of more than one biological modality, no technology can capture all modalities within the same cell. For current data integration methods, the feasibility of cross-modal integration relies on the existence of highly correlated, a priori 'linked' features. We describe matching X-modality via fuzzy smoothed embedding (MaxFuse), a cross-modal data integration method that, through iterative coembedding, data smoothing and cell matching, uses all information in each modality to obtain high-quality integration even when features are weakly linked. MaxFuse is modality-agnostic and demonstrates high robustness and accuracy in the weak linkage scenario, achieving 20~70% relative improvement over existing methods under key evaluation metrics on benchmarking datasets. A prototypical example of weak linkage is the integration of spatial proteomic data with single-cell sequencing data. On two example analyses of this type, MaxFuse enabled the spatial consolidation of proteomic, transcriptomic and epigenomic information at single-cell resolution on the same tissue section.

Recent technological advances have enabled analyses of the proteome and metabolome, transcriptome, and various aspects of the epigenome such as methylation, histone modification and chromatin accessibility within individual cells. In addition to technologies operating on dissociated single cells, rapid progress has been made on the in situ measurement of transcriptome, proteome, epigenome and other modalities on tissue sections at single-cell or close to single-cell resolution, retaining the spatial context. To harness the new technologies and growing data resources for biological discovery, a primary challenge is the reliable integration of data across modalities. Cross-modal integration, also referred to as ‘diagonal integration’, is the alignment of single cells or spatial spots across datasets where different features (or modalities) are profiled in each dataset. Despite the progress, key limitations still hinder reliable cross-modal integration. A key factor limiting the accuracy of existing methods is the strength of linkage between modalities. A feature is ‘linked’ between two modalities if it was measured in, or can be predicted by, both modalities. Most existing methods are designed for scenarios where there is a large number of linked features that also exhibit strong cross-modality correlations, a situation that we refer to as 'strong linkage'. Cross-modality integration in scenarios of weak linkage, where the number of linked features is small and/or the between-modality correlation for the linked features is weak, is especially challenging. A prototypical example of weak linkage is between targeted protein assays and transcriptome or epigenome assays such as scRNA-seq or scATAC-seq.

We developed a method that we call MaxFuse, a model-free, adaptable method that can accurately integrate data across weakly linked modalities. The input to MaxFuse are data from two modalities in the form of two pairs of matrices. For convenience, we can call the two modalities Y and Z. First, we have a pair of cell-by-feature matrices that contain all measured features in each modality (all-feature matrices). In addition, we represent the initial knowledge about the linkage between the two modalities as another pair of cell-by-feature matrices whose columns have one-to-one correspondences (linked-feature matrices). During stage 1 of the MaxFuse pipeline, cell-cell similarities are identified within each modality and initial cross-modal matching of cells is performed. This stage consists of three major steps: (1) computing a fuzzy nearest-neighbor graph for each modality based on all features; (2) boosting the signal-to-noise ratio in the linked features via 'fuzzy smoothing'; (3) computing distances between all cross-modal cell pairs based on the smoothed linked features and performing an initial matching via linear assignment. Stage 2 improves matching quality by iterating the sequence of joint embedding, fuzzy smoothing and linear assignment steps. Starting with the initial matches, MaxFuse learns a linear joint embedding (e.g., Canonical Correlation Analysis - CCA) of cells across modalities. Coordinates of this joint embedding are treated as new linked features and fuzzy smoothing is applied. MaxFuse updates the cell-matching by applying linear assignment on the pairwise distances of these fuzzy-smoothed joint embedding coordinates. This iteration continues until convergence. In stage 3, MaxFuse screens the matched pairs from the last iteration, retaining high-quality matches as pivots. These pivots are used to compute a final joint embedding and to propagate matches to unmatched cells.

We benchmarked MaxFuse on a CITE-seq dataset of PBMCs with 228 protein markers and whole transcriptome. For comparison, we applied Seurat(V3), Liger, Harmony and BindSC. Based on cell-type-level label transfer accuracy and cell-level matching accuracy (FOSCTTM, FOSKNN), MaxFuse was superior by a sizable margin. With a reduced panel of 30 antibodies, MaxFuse successfully mitigated the effect of reduced panel size on integration quality, achieving approximately 90% accuracy for level 1 annotation, whereas accuracy of the other methods ranged from around 15% to 75%. We further benchmarked MaxFuse on additional single-cell multiome datasets: CITE-seq (Bone Marrow, 25 antibodies), Ab-seq (Bone Marrow, 97 antibodies), ASAP-seq (PBMC, 227 antibodies + ATAC), and TEA-seq (PBMC, 46 antibodies + ATAC). Over all benchmarks, MaxFuse outperformed other methods, often by a sizable margin. For integration of strongly linked modalities (scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq), MaxFuse achieved best or close-to-best integration performance among tested methods (Maestro, scJoint, scGLUE) and was computationally much faster. For example, for a dataset of 20,000 cells, MaxFuse completed within 5 min on a MacBook Pro, while scGLUE took hours.

MaxFuse enables information-rich spatial pattern discovery. We evaluated its performance on integrating a CODEX multiplex imaging dataset (46 markers) with scRNA-seq data of human tonsils. MaxFuse produced an embedding that integrated the two modalities while preserving cell population structure, achieving high label transfer accuracy (93.3%) compared to other methods which failed to preserve distinctions. We reconstructed spatial patterns of gene expression relative to the Germinal Center (GC) boundary. For GC-specific transcripts (BCL6, AICDA, FOXP1), we observed high expression within the boundary and a sharp drop outside. For memory-related transcripts (CCR6, BANK1, FCER2), we saw a gradual increase outside the GC. These patterns were consistent with RNAscope validation. Furthermore, MaxFuse was utilized for automated cell-type annotation of CODEX cells via label transfer, achieving nearly 90% accuracy, substantially improving upon direct annotation methods (CELESTA, Astir). Finally, we applied MaxFuse to a tri-modal atlas-level integration of HUBMAP data (CODEX, snRNA-seq, snATAC-seq of human intestine). By chaining pairwise alignments (Protein-RNA and RNA-ATAC), we constructed high-resolution spatial maps of full transcriptome RNA expression and transcription factor binding site accessibility. For example, CD163 protein, RNA, and gene activity scores all localized to the same spatial positions in macrophages. Spatial enrichment of transcription factor motif accessibility (e.g., IRF4, KLF4, SRF) was also mapped.

MaxFuse relies on two key processes to overcome weak linkage: (1) fuzzy smoothing, which denoises linked features by leveraging the full feature set via nearest-neighbor graphs; and (2) iterative refinement, which uses all information in both modalities through iterative cycles of coembedding, graph-smoothing and matching. This ensures that information from all features is used to generate the final matching. MaxFuse substantially improves upon state-of-the-art methods for integrating data from targeted protein assays with transcriptome/epigenome assays. For strong linkage scenarios, MaxFuse accuracy was comparable to scGLUE but at a considerably lower computational cost. The MaxFuse pipeline involves: Input preparation with all-feature and linked-feature matrices; Meta-cell construction to alleviate sparsity; Fuzzy smoothing defined as $\bar{Y}_{m}^{\circ}=\mathcal{S}_{Y}\left(Y_{m}^{\circ}; w_{0}\right)$ and $\widetilde{Z}_{m}^{\circ}=\mathcal{S}_{Z}\left(Z_{m}^{\circ}; w_{0}\right)$ with $\mathcal{S}_Y(A; w)=w A+(1-w)\mathcal{A}_Y(A)$; Initial matching via linear assignment problem $\text{minimize } \langle\Pi, D^{\circ}\rangle$; Cross-modality joint embedding and iterative refinement using CCA; Propagation of matching and postprocessing to get final single-cell matching and joint embedding $Y^{e}=\hat{Y}_{y}^{e}\in R^{N_{y}\times r^{e}}$ and $Z^{e}=Z\widehat{C}_{z}^{e}\in R^{N_{z}\times r^{e}}$.
